title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure projects including awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, authorizing a water/sewer connection agreement for a Radkkey Avenue duplex, approving a $94,860 construction agreement with Rothschild for Military Road improvements, and selecting construction service consultants for upcoming road projects. The committee also discussed strategies to address sanitary sewer violations from residents illegally discharging sump pump water into the sewer system.

agenda.item: Call to order
Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comment
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
CIP update
Street operation update
Utility operating update
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC annual report
Resolution 2026-014 transportation funding
Recycled asphalt base course production award
Sanitary sewer discharge violation discussion
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue
Military Road construction agreement
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project will likely move to 2028. He noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion included updates on Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Schofield Avenue punch list items with HOS Construction, and the retention pond phosphorus modeling showing increased benefit for Weston but decreased benefit for Rothschild.

Staff reported on spring activities including removing road limits with Marathon County, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week, curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, and storm damage cleanup from East Nick. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Vehicle maintenance included removing plow equipment and servicing mowers. The committee discussed ongoing flooding issues near Zenzer Road and potential permanent pump station solutions.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and meter card maintenance. Sanitary lift stations experienced high activity due to clear water infiltration. Data showed April treated 53.7 million gallons versus 37 million gallons pumped into the system - indicating roughly 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. Staff identified sump pump discharges as a major contributor, with one Kathleen lift station house causing continuous pumping. The in-pipe technologies I&I study will help identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, similar to 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. Utility value reached nearly $34 million, an increase of 6.7% due to infrastructure including Wells 7 and 8 and water main from the Weston Avenue project. The utility serves 6,100 customers total with 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. PFAS settlement funds of $1.44 million were received. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael presented a League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supporting long-term transportation funding solutions. He noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and that Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership. Motion to adopt resolution 2026-014 passed unanimously.

Michael explained the Ryan Street recycling operation where concrete and asphalt are crushed into base course. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton versus $4.25/ton from competitor. Estimated 30,000 tons equals $126,900. Analysis showed savings of nearly $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau's costs, totaling approximately $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on fining residents who illegally discharge sump pumps into sanitary sewers. The municipal code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee members distinguished between unknowing violations (which should receive notice first) versus willful violations (higher fines). Discussion included requiring proof of correction after violations. The committee directed staff to create enforcement language starting at $100/day for violations. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement for a duplex at 2230 Radkkey Avenue to temporarily connect via long lateral service until Radkkey Avenue reconstruction. Committee member Zagami requested adding a date requirement, settling on 'no later than 2029' for reconstruction. The property owner will pay assessment when mains are extended. Motion to recommend approval with the 2029 deadline passed unanimously.

The agreement with Village of Rothschild covers Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Steam Construction is the contractor. Weston's share for utility work (sanitary sewer $6,156 and water main $88,704) totals $94,860. Water main must be relocated due to storm sewer improvements. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik intersection roundabout: MSA at $37,315 (recommended) versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue: MSA at $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730 (recommended due to full-time experienced staff versus MSA's summer intern). Motion to select MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations at $100/day fine
Recommend village board approve Radkkey Avenue water/sewer connection agreement with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Recommend village board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860
Select MSA for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction services and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue project
Send resolution 2026-014 supporting transportation funding to governor and legislators
Schedule special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue on May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday this week
Consider streetlighting for Schofield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road in 2030 project
Staff to calculate cost impact of 15 million gallons clear water infiltration for next meeting

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially boosting attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Election of officers - Chair
Election of officers - Vice Chair
Election of officers - Secretary
Public comments
Minutes of previous meeting - April 21st
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members
Adjournment

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated and elected as Vice Chair, with Jackson providing the second. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously with no opposition.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved unanimously. The motion was seconded by Renee.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented the grant request for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole event in the area. Commission members noted the request showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission discussed needing better room tracking data from hotels. A motion to approve $5,000 (matching last year's grant) was made and passed unanimously, with Jackson seconding. The commission requested better room usage documentation in future reports.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees and attended South Dakota State. The candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide better hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Drive, and adopted a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with all members present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Open public comment period
Approval of April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive (taken out of order as Item 6)
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District (Item 5)
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
April 2026 building permits
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Commissioner remarks
Future agenda items
Adjournment

discussions.item: Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline easement running through the property. Police Chief Jeremy explained the building is designed for future growth from current 40 officers to 50-60 capacity. Discussion included an alternate storage shed placement, with commissioners suggesting it be located further north to allow for future building expansion. The motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Jennifer presented the proposed inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection focused on material handling. Staff developed a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Discussion revealed one existing facility near Sternberg Road has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process despite being closest to the village well. Commissioners agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Motion to approve passed unanimously with addition that staff report back to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

Jennifer announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. She agreed to send email notification and calendar invites to commissioners.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via remote connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing previous flexibility shown for the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reports on the salt storage shed inspection program to ensure complaints don't get 'lost in the sauce.'
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for implementation in wellhead protection district
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg Road that never went through conditional use process
Joint steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be addressed in fall agenda

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new and returning members, featuring presentations from department heads of the Health Department, Veteran Services Office, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received educational overviews of departmental missions, budgets, and key initiatives including homelessness response, veteran suicide prevention, and meals on wheels programs.

agenda.item: Call to order and notation of remote attendance by Supervisors Kremes, Vice Chair Rosenberg, and Chair Boots
Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute, ordinance, resolution or policy - no items
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department overview presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Services Office presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging & Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date confirmation and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's structure under section 2.04 sub 5 of county ordinances, detailing reporting relationships with the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, North Central Health Care, and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He explained the roles of subordinate bodies including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will be working on a new strategic plan expected to be completed by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's 37.6 FTE staff across four divisions: environmental health (overseeing 1,200+ licenses), nursing/communicable disease, community health improvement, and administration. She highlighted new initiatives including public health vending machines with free tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates, a new homelessness system response position, and lead mitigation efforts. Administrator Leonard noted the department's $5 million budget is approximately half tax levy, with significant revenue from public charges for services and intergovernmental grants.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa (not present), describing the department's nearly 140 FTE staff and $22 million budget with about $7 million in tax levy. He highlighted the Family Keys housing stability pilot program as very successful in addressing root causes of housing instability among child welfare and youth justice cases. Leonard indicated potential future restructuring requests due to increased need in areas like child support and children's long-term support.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported his office increased from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE and saw customer interactions jump from 5,900 to 8,149 in 2025. He reported veterans in Marathon County receive $44 million annually in VA benefits, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025 alone. Galindo highlighted priorities including surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention (noting 7 of Marathon County's 30 suicides in 2025 were veterans). He is exploring the Staff Sergeant Gordon Fox VA suicide prevention grant. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have a VSO and described good working relationships with surrounding counties.

Executive Director Mike Ray described the four-county ADRC (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) serving as a one-stop shop for aging and disability services with about 60 FTEs and 385-400 volunteers. He highlighted significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs up 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat due to the Older Americans Act not being reauthorized for over two years. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135. The regional model hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024. Ray shared a recent example of a volunteer driver finding a fallen client who needed emergency services.

actionItems: Approved April 1st committee meeting minutes unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report data to the full board
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan information to future meetings
Next meeting scheduled for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3 PM (corrected from originally stated May 29th)
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with items for future agendas

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made committee appointments.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public comments
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Acknowledge reports from departments
Work product transmittals including building permits and budget status
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Remote meeting attendance policy amendments
Committee appointments and farmers market discussion

discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning whether real cost data exists to justify the fees. Trustee Clark supported the fees noting population growth requires more park infrastructure. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance creates a reciprocal inspection agreement between Safer and municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others. Once a food truck is inspected by any participating department, a sticker is placed on the vehicle eliminating the need for multiple inspections. Ordinance 26-008 was approved with motion by Lope, second by Zagami, passing unanimously.

Significant debate occurred over policy language. The board discussed removing specific health/safety/welfare justification requirements, with some trustees wanting broader discretion for chairs to approve remote attendance. An amendment was made by Trustee Beck, seconded by Clark, to strike sentences requiring specific justifications - passed 5-2 with Hermling and one other voting no. The original motion as amended then passed 5-2 with the same dissenting votes.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Location at Kennedy Park
discussions.body: Discussion-only item addressing the market's relocation from the Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue location after the property owner decided to develop that site. Trustee Olsen raised safety concerns about vehicles parking on grass along Alderson Street. Trustee Beck questioned operating costs but staff confirmed the market operates at no loss. Renee explained the market grew from 25 stalls at the original location to 53 spots at Kennedy Park, and vendors appreciate having vehicles with them. No action was taken; the board acknowledged this is an evolving situation.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, passed unanimously. Parks, Finance/HR, and CLIPS still have openings. Board agreed that committee/commission applications should be included in board packets going forward.

Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street requested secondhand article and jewelry dealer licenses. Chief Hunt explained these businesses deal with large dollar amounts in precious metals requiring tracking through a police-accessible database. Motion by Hermling, second by Clark, was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Passed unanimously as amended.

Public Works Director Dolan reported that a DOT engineering study rated the road at 35 mph, but traffic data showed the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph. A second speed study is needed but was delayed due to construction. Trustee noted the current posted speed is inconsistent with the village ordinance. Staff will bring this back to the board, possibly at a joint meeting.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street offered public comment on two topics. He criticized the village for spending an estimated $108,000 on outside engineering consultants when an engineering technician position remains unfilled, calling for administrator accountability. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer meeting before being officially appointed by the board, and called for more transparency. During the impact fee hearing, Pitel questioned whether the village has actual cost data to justify the fee increases.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection ordinance adopted
E-bike regulations ordinance adopted
Nuisance ordinance amendments adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Schofield Coin and Hobby pending background check and occupancy permit
Comprehensive Plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to bring Weston Avenue speed limit issue back to board
Staff directed to include committee/commission applications in board packets
Staff assisting Town of Ringle with building inspections - intergovernmental agreement to be developed

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board approved committee appointments for the new term, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee at-large positions, and unanimously passed a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris following severe weather that damaged the Ringle area. The board suspended its rules to consider the emergency tipping fee resolution directly from the Solid Waste Management Board.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of the notice and roll call
Committee appointments by County Board Chair
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10-C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26: Social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees
Announcements and adjournment

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspective, developing leadership among newer supervisors (34% in first or second term), and respecting supervisor preferences. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who cited her advocacy for children and elders), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). After paper ballot voting, Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to Landfill Tipping Fees
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for storm debris following severe weather that caused significant damage in Ringle, Kronenwetter, and other areas. The state waived $13.10/ton and Ringle waived $2.33/ton. The county proposed reducing fees to $36.94/ton (from $65 standard rate) to cover costs. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak and requested the county waive fees entirely, citing that approximately 44 homes need demolition and insurance adjusters haven't yet visited many properties. After extensive discussion about enterprise fund operations, precedent concerns, and potential community foundation partnership for unreimbursed costs, the board unanimously suspended rules to consider the resolution and then passed it unanimously.

publicComment: No formal public comment period was held, but Supervisor Rosenberg requested special dispensation for two residents to speak on the tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty spoke for three minutes, asking the county to waive fees entirely for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle had waived their fees and that private businesses had donated up to $40,000 in cleanup efforts. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes need demolition, requesting fee waivers for underinsured homeowners and more time since many insurance adjusters haven't yet arrived.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the new term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 24-26 approved increasing out of home placement reserve
Resolution 25-26 approved amending social services budget
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to $36.94/ton for certified storm debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to work with community foundation on mechanism for reimbursing unreimbursed insurance costs for landowners
Solid waste management board to develop clear policy within six months for handling future disaster debris situations
Staff to continue tracking certified storm debris and monitor if extension beyond 60 days is needed

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only cover septic system reconnect fees (not other zoning or pouts fees) for properties impacted by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from three county departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public comment
Approval of March 31, 2026 committee minutes
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation, Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck seconding. The motion carried with no opposition.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential on behalf of property owners James Offer and David Tract. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pogle explained there is a home and horse riding stable that are non-conforming uses in conservancy, and rezoning would make them conforming uses. The property is home, barns, stables, and pasture - no natural area being preserved. Town of McMillan recommended approval April 20, 2026. No public testimony. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented staff recommendation to waive county zoning and pouts fees for tornado-impacted properties. County Administrator Lance clarified this follows the governor's emergency declaration. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck questioned fairness to individuals affected by disasters not declared emergencies. Eric Vogel asked if homeowners insurance would cover fees. Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees (not other zoning or pouts fees), seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Amendment passed. Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the amended resolution to county board, seconded by Supervisor Lam. Motion carried but not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher presented department overview including the landfill history, tipping fee breakdown, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, leachate treatment challenges, and upcoming capital projects including $6 million cell construction and on-site leachate treatment facility. He noted lithium-ion batteries cause weekly fires at the facility. Marilyn Bend commented the county is fortunate to have the landfill to keep private facilities' prices in line.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie. The department operates Wausau and Marathon County park systems since 1924, manages 30,700 acres of county forest, and has 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees. Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as the new county forest administrator.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) to Marathon County Board of Supervisors for consideration
Forward James Offer/David Tract rezoning petition approval to county board
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition approval to county board
Town of Green Valley should update comprehensive plan to reflect zoning change if applicable
Town of McMillan should update comprehensive plan to reflect zoning change if applicable
Track costs and requests related to fee waiver implementation for future policy development

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center to next month due to the chair's absence, approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Plover River, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising progress and trail maintenance challenges. Public comment included opposition to dogs at the pool and criticism of the 4 PM meeting time and Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Approval of minutes from April 27, 2026
Public comments
Kennedy Park update
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs discussion and action
Topics for future meetings
Staff remarks and announcements

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved with a motion and second. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Sean Oster Bring presented a timeline document tracking the Kennedy Park master plan from approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked about fundraising timelines, and Oster Bring clarified there's a 10-year horizon with no hard deadlines. The Friends of Kennedy Park plan to announce fundraising totals at their June tournament. Oster Bring reported the group has approximately $84,000-$85,000 at the community foundation for field lights, with a goal of $117,000. Youth Baseball contributed $8,700 for field equipment and made a $15,000 contribution as 'first dollar in.' Acting chair noted the Friends group deserves 'grace' as volunteers and encouraged community involvement.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and maps showing fallen trees removed from the river and the installation of protective wood mats over gas pipelines for equipment access. The mats were provided by TC Energy (formerly ANR pipeline/ATC). Dan explained the mats were used to create access for logging equipment during frozen conditions, with staff building snow bridges across ditches.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the June meeting because Chair Katrina, who requested the agenda item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy (6504 [street unclear]) spoke during public comment opposing dogs at the pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' The acting chair assured Andy his objection would be noted and invited him to return next month.

Staff discussed challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches along the trail, noting the best removal tool (sandblaster/pressure washer) cannot access the area due to narrow bridges and steep terrain. Committee member suggested anti-vandalism coatings or seal coats. Laro suggested organizing volunteer work days, noting many people love the trail. Staff mentioned the benches originally arrived via a volunteer team-building day with village directors. No formal action was taken; staff will explore cleaning options.

Dan from Marathon County Parks presented version 7 of signage for Plover River kayak launches. Signs will be installed at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks landings in Weston, plus locations in Schofield. Signs include QR codes linking to the river gauge and Marathon County Parks Foundation donation page. Dan noted the Highway J launch may close for the season due to bridge replacement. The committee approved a motion to install the signs and forward to the village board for approval. Motion carried unanimously.

Staff requested discussion of food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds after receiving an email request. Signs would encourage keeping food at picnic tables rather than play equipment. Committee agreed to bring the item back for discussion, with Oster Bring noting 'we don't want to spend time on it if we all of a sudden bring it to you and you're like, what are you doing?'

publicComment: Two residents spoke. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) opposed allowing dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs belong in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), speaking remotely under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 PM meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned Kennedy Park financial reporting, calling the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental' and asking about missing grant money and glow event proceeds. He suggested the Friends of Kennedy Park might be 'frauds' for not sharing detailed finances.
actionItems: Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J; forwarded to village board for approval
Staff to explore cleaning options and anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail benches and structures
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan process beginning with kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 PM in training room

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved minutes from the previous meeting and acknowledged April budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on clerk's office operations, including elections, licensing, tax collection, and the village newsletter. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
April budget status report
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Legal details report for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Staff remarks and farmers market update
Committee member announcements and adjournment

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion passed with all in favor.

A committee member asked about final numbers for TIF closure. Staff explained they are anticipating October/November for bond payoff, and the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds. Staff confirmed the village will be able to pay off the debt as planned.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an extensive overview of the clerk's office operations. The office now has two full-time employees (Pam and Sarah) and one part-time employee (Leah), down from four full-time staff previously. Discussion covered election administration including Badger Books ($11,000 for four units), postage costs jumping from $6,000 to $15,000 for presidential election years, and poll worker wages. Committee discussed chief inspector pay being on the lower end at $13/hour compared to county average, with Pam indicating she will likely request an increase to $15/hour for the next election term.

Pam explained the pet licensing fee increase from $10 to $20, noting Marathon County requested municipalities raise fees because they pay about $100,000 to the Humane Society annually but were only collecting $65,000 from municipalities. Discussion revealed the village paid $175 per mouse for eight mice picked up under the non-dog stray contract. A committee member suggested requiring law enforcement to bring in strays to control costs.

The quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually ($9,000 postage, $20,000 printing) to reach 5,089 postal patrons. Committee discussed alternatives including email distribution and coordinating printing bids with other municipalities. Staff noted they previously tried eliminating mailed newsletters but residents requested they return.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park is going well. She has been present for 6-7 hours each Saturday since opening. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and some early produce. Vendors reported being very happy with the setup and location. Committee discussed parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets during market days and concurrent baseball/track events.

Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve. The chair noted the committee now has open spots and encouraged members to recruit interested citizens.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIF bonds, anticipated summer 2025 for October payoff
Clerk to consider requesting chief inspector wage increase to $15/hour for next election term
Staff to continue monitoring parking situation at farmers market and Kennedy Park events
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to the nuisance ordinance, recommended a new e-bike ordinance with restrictions on emotos, and authorized demolition of a condemned property on Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring decisions on both items pending additional research.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comments
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Department reports - Code enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police
Proposed amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances
E-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82
Section 82.107 overnight parking regulations
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street
Mobile food vendors fire inspection ordinance
Address corrections for E911 compliance
4104 Shore Avenue raze request

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke expressing concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, stating his opinion that the execution is 'terrible' and predicting the grassy areas will be destroyed. He questioned whether village code allows parking on grass in parks, noted ADA parking spaces are placed on grass instead of asphalt which may not comply with requirements, questioned the need for new fire inspections on food trucks, and criticized the age of some code violation cases on the enforcement list.

Code Enforcement reported 3020 Mountain View Avenue will come off the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and the new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for all three municipalities. The reports were acknowledged unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to the nuisance ordinance, including switching tree limb height requirements to 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways, and adding language about compliance with jurisdictions having authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the board of trustees. Passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred about the proposed e-bike ordinance. Chief Hunt strongly advocated for prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bikes) on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion addressed whether electric scooters should be included in restrictions, with committee members debating whether to exempt children's play scooters. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with the addition that emotos are prohibited on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property in the village. Passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston has a 12-hour no-parking window (4am-4pm, October 31-May 1) while surrounding municipalities have 3.5-5 hour windows. Public Works Director Michael noted staff rarely plows residential streets before 7-8am, so overnight restrictions don't help operations. Chief Hunt advocated keeping overnight parking restrictions to give officers time to clear vehicles before morning plowing. The committee agreed to defer the item until July, with Michael to identify specific problem areas that could be signed individually.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest High School parking lot. Committee members questioned whether the church should pay for installation rather than the village. Public Works Director estimated $2,000-3,000 for a basic crosswalk versus $25,000-30,000 for a lighted system. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend board approval if Mount Olive is willing to cover the installation cost. Passed unanimously.

Fire Chief presented a regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston where one inspection is valid for all participating municipalities. A recent inspection found one vendor with significant code violations including improper galvanized steel LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8. Passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing corrections needed for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing two addresses), 3917 Ross Avenue (out of sequence and has secondary unit), and 2211 Radkey Avenue (new duplex with out-of-sequence apartment complex nearby). Motion made and seconded (Olsen/Campkey) to recommend approval to board of trustees. Passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure that has been posted since 2021 and abandoned since 2010. A previous fire training burn was canceled due to unsafe conditions. The structure was found being used as a chicken coop. Current owners are up to date on taxes and have agreed to the demolition, with costs to be placed on the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to authorize staff to begin the raze order. Passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment expressing opposition to the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, citing concerns about grass damage from vehicle parking, ADA accessibility issues with handicap spaces on grass instead of asphalt, questioning the need for food truck fire inspections, and criticizing the lack of progress on older code enforcement cases.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to board of trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance to board with addition prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property
Public Works Director to identify specific streets requiring parking restrictions and bring back to July meeting
Public Works Director to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to board of trustees
Authorize staff to begin raze order proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Chief Hunt to investigate addressing issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski
Staff to investigate property condition at Sternberg and South Timber intersection

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Preliminary Discussion Regarding Potential Litigation 19.85 (g)
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Call to Order
Roll Call
Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Reading of the Mission Statement
Excellence in Action: Maine Elementary
Public and Student Comment
Approve Consent Agenda (Action Requested)
Old/Recurring Business
New Business
Open Forum
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Adjourn

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Arbor Day Proclamation
Week of the Young Child Proclamation
National Preservation Month Proclamation
Workers Memorial Day Proclamation
March 24, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
April 14, 2026 Regular Common Council Minutes
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
